title: Some further progress on Zilber-Pink in Y(1)3
speaker: Christopher Daw
abstract: I will discuss work in progress with M. Orr (Manchester) and G. Papas (Weizmann) on the Zilber-Pink conjecture for $Y(1)^3$. This is known for so-called asymmetric curves by the 2012 work of Habegger-Pila. More recently, an approach known as the G-function method, has yielded further cases, namely, curves intersecting $(\infty,\infty,\infty)$ (D-Orr) and curves intersecting a special point in the boundary (Papas). In this work, we extend the method to deal with curves intersecting a boundary modular curve, and to give an unconditional result for points with few places of supersingular reduction.
tags: Workshop on Special Cycles and Related Topics

proof_stages.label: Zilber-Pink as an arithmetic point-intersection problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk states the general Zilber-Pink unlikely-intersections conjecture and emphasizes that positive-dimensional intersections are geometrically well understood, while point intersections remain an arithmetic bottleneck except in special CM or Hecke-type cases.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to modular points on curves in Y(1)^3
proof_stages.summary: The speaker specializes to a curve in Y(1)^3, classifies special subvarieties via CM-coordinate and modular-polynomial relations, reviews known cases, and identifies the remaining problem as finiteness of points satisfying two modular-polynomial relations.

proof_stages.label: Boundary-modular and supersingular-exponent theorems
proof_stages.summary: The new joint results are formulated: Zilber-Pink holds when the closure of the curve contains a boundary-modular point, and finitely many remaining modular points exist under a bound on the number of nearby supersingular reduction places.

proof_stages.label: Height-bound reduction by Pila-Zannier and isogeny estimates
proof_stages.summary: Combining the Pila-Zannier strategy with isogeny estimates reduces the desired finiteness statements to proving a logarithmic Weil-height bound in terms of a positive power of the field of definition, allowing small complexity factors.

proof_stages.label: Renormalized periods as G-functions and the Hasse principle
proof_stages.summary: For an abelian scheme over a curve, local period matrices are normalized at a base point to produce G-functions, and the André-Chudnovsky Hasse principle bounds algebraic arguments at which these functions satisfy nontrivial global relations.

proof_stages.label: Isogeny-induced global relations for elliptic pairs
proof_stages.summary: Applying the G-function apparatus to two elliptic schemes with a base-point isogeny, the proof derives trace relations from isogenies at other points and checks at archimedean, good-reduction, and bad-reduction places that these yield rational relations whose product is nontrivial and global.

proof_stages.label: Boundary proof via bounded bad-reduction places
proof_stages.summary: In the boundary-modular situation, projection to two coordinates and the degenerating third coordinate force the relevant finite places to be bad-reduction places of a fixed elliptic curve, keeping the degree of the global relation uniformly bounded and yielding the height bound.

proof_stages.label: Interior three-family argument and supersingular obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Using all three elliptic families gives a place-independent relation at ordinary finite places because ordinary endomorphism rings are commutative, while supersingular places still cause degree growth, so the theorem assumes their number is sufficiently small.

examples.name: Boundary line over a CM point
examples.note: A fiber or line in the boundary over a CM point is mentioned to illustrate why a broader statement involving arbitrary special curves in the boundary would not control the remaining coordinate.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only cites a boundary fiber/line over a CM point as a case that would be included in a desired boundary-special-curve statement, without computing with it or checking any property on it.

passes.reason: A line over a CM point in the compactification boundary has a cuspidal/limiting coordinate and is cited as an obstruction to a broader “hit any special boundary curve” statement because the remaining coordinate is uncontrolled.

It is a coordinate fibre such as {∞}×{CM}×Y(1), a limiting boundary stratum with a degenerate cusp coordinate, and it is cited as the obstruction to treating arbitrary special boundary curves because the free coordinate is uncontrolled.
It is a cusp/boundary coordinate-fiber over a CM point, so a degenerate limiting special curve, and it is used as the obstruction to extending the statement to all special boundary curves.
It is a cusp-boundary limiting fiber with one coordinate fixed at a CM point, cited as the obstruction to the stronger formulation using any special curve in the boundary.
A line such as {cusp}×{CM point}×Y(1) is a cuspidal limiting boundary fiber, and it is invoked as the obstructive case to a hoped-for statement covering all special boundary curves.
A boundary fiber with one coordinate fixed at a CM point is a limiting boundary stratum, cited as an obstruction to the stronger statement that meeting any special boundary curve should suffice.

A fiber line over a CM point in the boundary is cited as an obstructive enemy case to a stronger special-curve-in-the-boundary statement, and it is a limiting cusp-boundary locus with a degenerate modular factor rather than a symmetric or sporadic object.

A cuspidal boundary fiber with one coordinate fixed at a CM point is a limiting/partly collapsed special boundary curve, invoked as an obstruction to the broader statement that meeting any special curve in the boundary should suffice.

It is used as an obstruction to the hoped-for statement about any special boundary curve, and structurally it is a cuspidal boundary fiber over a CM point rather than a sporadic or maximally symmetric object.

A boundary fiber/coordinate line over a CM point is used as an obstruction to the stronger hoped-for statement about meeting any special boundary curve, but structurally it is an ordinary special coordinate fiber rather than sporadic, extremal, degenerate, or maximally symmetric.

Used as an obstruction to the tempting statement covering all special boundary curves, and structurally it is a limiting boundary/cusp line with one coordinate fixed at a CM point, not a sporadic or maximally symmetric object.
A boundary fiber {CM point}×Y(1) is a limiting/collapsed special curve in the compactification, invoked as an obstruction to extending the boundary theorem to arbitrary special curves.
